data.title: A Golden Buy-The-Drop Opportunity In Best-Of-Breed Dividend Stocks
data.description:
Dividend stocks, especially those that consistently grow their payouts, are often priced at a premium. This is because a company that pays out sustainable income and consistently grows it over the long term generally also compounds shareholder returns consistently year after year. This makes them not only attractive income machines that can pay out attractive current yields that grow at a rate that may or may not beat inflation over time, but also ones that can compound well through very attractive total returns over the long term.

Currently, the market is offering several very attractive opportunities to buy these quality dividend growth machines at deeply discounted valuations. In this article, I'm going to detail two best-of-breed dividend stocks that I think are golden buy opportunities.

A Premier Apartment REIT at a Deep Discount

The first option I'm going to discuss is Camden Property Trust (CPT). It is a multi-family REIT (VNQ) similar to peers like AvalonBay Communities, Inc. (AVB) and Mid-America Apartment Communities, Inc. (MAA) that owns over 58,000 apartment homes across 15 major US markets. It is heavily concentrated in high-growth, high-demand metros, including in the Sun Belt. These include areas like Houston, Dallas, Atlanta, Washington, DC, Southern California, Nashville, and Southeast Florida.

CPT operates and attracts high-income earners, as its average resident earns $118,000 per year and pays only about 19% of its income toward rent, which is one of the lowest rent-to-income ratios in the entire sector. This indicates that it has significant pricing power and a low probability of defaults on its leases. Recently, the company has been selling several of its California assets, with between $1.5 billion and $2 billion worth of assets on sale that it expects to close in the middle of this year.

The proceeds are expected to be reinvested into a combination of Sun Belt 1031 exchange purchases and a fairly aggressive allocation towards stock buybacks to arbitrage the steep discounting that the stock currently trades at. The company has already executed the vast majority of these stock buybacks, indicating that management is truly serious about share purchases and believes very strongly that its stock is undervalued. CPT does not expect any material accretion or dilution from this capital recycling in the near term. However, it believes that reinvesting in its undervalued stock and investing in the higher-growth Sun Belt markets will set the company up for much stronger performance over the long term once rents inflect in Sun Belt markets.

CPT expects strong growth recovery in markets like Atlanta, Dallas, Nashville, and Austin to help reignite growth for the company in the near future. Presently, the dividend yield is quite attractive at over 4% on a next-12-month basis. The fact that it has grown its dividend for five straight years and its dividend is very well covered, with just a 63% core FFO payout ratio, along with the considerable stock buyback this year, should all enable the company to continue growing its dividend moving forward.

Meanwhile, the 19% discount to net asset value that CPT currently trades at means that it offers investors an attractive combination of a mid-single-digit yield, strong long-term growth potential, and material valuation multiple expansion potential once sentiment in the sector improves.

Risks & The Bull Case

Of course, it is not a risk-free investment thesis, as the company is still facing weakness in many of its markets in terms of rent growth, which is keeping growth subdued overall. However, in the long term, these assets are high quality and generally located in quality markets, which means that eventually their growth should improve.

The real asset nature of CPT's assets means that they should also be a valuable inflation hedge over the long term. While investors get an attractive dividend deal that is well covered and should continue growing for the foreseeable future, it is also supported by a very strong balance sheet, a deep discount to private market values, and a company that is aggressively arbitraging its discount to NAV with stock buybacks. Thus, I see this as a heads-I-win, tails-I-don't-lose-much investment proposition.

A Rapidly Growing Alternative Asset Giant

The other opportunity I'm going to discuss today is an alternative asset manager called Ares Management Corporation (ARES). As I recently detailed elsewhere, I am a major bull on ARES's peers like Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL)—with my bull thesis detailed here—and Blackstone Inc. (BX)—with my bull thesis detailed here. However, I also think ARES is a very attractive opportunity, with well over $600 billion in assets under management and very strong fundraising in 2025 that has put it on the list of one of the world's largest alternative asset managers, with diversification across private credit, real assets, infrastructure, real estate, and several other product offerings.

The company has recently made acquisitions that position ARES as one of the world's top three owners and operators of industrial real estate and also gave it digital infrastructure capabilities. With a broad-based business like that, even in the face of private credit headwinds with increased withdrawal requests, ARES is still poised to grow very strongly and build on its 25% year-over-year management fees that it posted last year.

Fee-related earnings grew by 30% in 2025, with 33% year-over-year growth in Q4. With $160 billion of uninvested AUM entering 2026, it also has enormous embedded growth that should enable it to continue generating very strong fee-related earnings growth even if fundraising slows considerably. However, management has guided that 2026 fundraising should be as good or even better than in 2025, with the fee-related earnings margin expected to expand by up to 150 basis points this year.

Why This 20% Earnings Growth Machine Is Now Irresistibly Cheap

Analyst consensus estimates currently project a 20% earnings per share CAGR through 2030 and a 17.5% dividend per share CAGR through 2030:

When you combine that with their recent 20% dividend hike for 2026, it is clear that management has a lot of confidence in the business. Additionally, the dividend yield is now over 4%, which makes it a very attractive combination of yield and growth and a very attractive buy on the recent significant sell-off. This is particularly true given that it now trades at a material discount to its historical average multiple:

Yes, it is true that ARES has a lot of exposure to private credit, and this is a risk to the business, but as evidenced by its public fund, Ares Capital (ARCC), which has a phenomenal long-term track record, ARES is one of the very best private credit investors in the world, and its portfolio is currently performing very well. As a result, it continues to raise immense amounts of money from institutional investors, the negative headlines notwithstanding.

Why These Are Golden Buy-The-Drop Opportunities

Between CPT and ARES, both businesses are currently facing some headwinds—between weakness in Sun Belt multifamily and private credit and negative market sentiment—however, I believe the long-term fundamentals remain firmly intact for both businesses, making them attractive buys on the dip for a combination of yield, growth, and valuation multiple expansion. These are the exact kind of names that we look to load up on in our 7-8% yielding, total-return-outperforming, and below-market-beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: HP Inc.: Near 52-Week Low, 6% Yield, Valuation Attractive Amid Dynamic Memory Environment
data.description:
Introduction

At a time when the market continues to make new highs, finding attractively priced stocks is becoming scarce. But there's always an opportunity somewhere.

In the case of HP Inc. (HPQ), their performance in the past 5 years has been disappointing. But with a forward P/E of less than 7.0x, HPQ's investment case is compelling considering the dividend yield above 6% and upside potential long-term.

While I remain cautious going forward, income investors with a higher tolerance for risk may find HPQ a worthy investment.

In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, why the stock has underperformed in the past 5 years, and why, despite my hold rating, HPQ could be attractive long-term.

Brief Overview

HP Inc. is a technology (XLK) hardware company that most of us are familiar with. They're known for their printers and personal computers, which we've likely used or still use in our place of work.

They operate in three segments: Printing, Personal Systems, and Corporate Investments/Other. Printing is the company's main growth driver.

HP Inc. has an extensive track record, having been founded in 1939 and going public in November of 1957. They separated from Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE) in 2015.

Latest Earnings/Disappointing Performance

HPQ reported their Q1 earnings in late February and managed to post a double beat. EPS of $0.81 beat by $0.04, while revenue of $14.4 billion beat by $510 million.

But both were down from the prior quarter's $14.6 billion and $0.93, respectively. So, results were underwhelming to say the least. On a positive note, both did see growth from the prior year's quarter, something I always look for when investing in a company.

While quarter-over-quarter growth is also important, looking out over a longer period is something I like to see, particularly with dividend-paying stocks. On an annual basis, EPS was up 9%, while revenue increased 7%.

This was driven by their Personal Systems segment, which saw revenue growth of 11%. But this was offset by a 2% decline in their Printing segment. As previously mentioned, Printing accounts for the largest portion of revenue at 19%.

This did improve from the 4.2% decline in Q4. But seeing a sequential decline is likely something that didn't sit well with investors. For 2025, revenue in the segment was down 3.7%, while Personal Systems continued its momentum, up 6.5% in 2025.

In Corporate, operating profits were negative at $281 million. But these improved from last year's negative $333 million. In Printing and Personal Systems, operating profit margins fell from 19% and 5.5%, respectively, to 18.3% and 5.0%.

So, from a performance standpoint, HPQ made some slight improvements, but headwinds still persisted. And this resulted in the stock being down double-digits in the past year.

But HPQ's problems have lingered for more than just the past year. When you look out over the last 5 years, which is the minimum data I like to analyze, you can see why the stock has performed the way it has since 2021.

You can see from the chart below that HPQ has been a disappointing investment, down over 39% compared to the S&P (SP500), up roughly 72%.

Deteriorating financials are to blame for the share price performance. The chart below shows net revenue, EPS, and operating profits in 2021 compared to 2024 and 2025.

2025

2024

2021

Net revenue

$55.3B

$53.6B

$63.5B

EPS

$3.12

$3.43

$3.79

Operating profits

$4.109B

$4.573B

$5.769B

While revenue did see 3% growth from the prior year, the 13% decline in the past 5 years raises concern. EPS was down 18%, while operating profits fell 29% from nearly $5.8 billion.

And going forward, headwinds are expected to persist. For Q2, management stated they expect earnings to again decline to a range of $0.70-0.76, a 10% decline using the midpoint from Q1.

Year-over-year, this represents a $0.02 increase. So, the company's lack of growth and underwhelming outlook are reasons for the huge underperformance.

HPQ has been bogged down by rising memory chip costs due to higher-for-longer inflation and tariffs. And although inflation and tariffs seem to have a clearer outlook on the direction going forward, I believe HPQ is unlikely to see any meaningful progress for at least another year or two.

Muted Growth Expected

And this is evident by their 2026 outlook. According to management, EPS projections at midpoint represent a 2.2% decline from 2025's $3.12. Free cash flow at the midpoint is flat from the $2.9 billion in FCF last year. For context, free cash flow 5 years ago was $4.2 billion.

Dividend Profile

Because of their deteriorating financials, HPQ's dividend yield above 6% makes a compelling investment case for income investors less risk averse. And despite the decline in free cash flow, the dividend was secured with a 37% payout ratio.

For 2025, HPQ paid $1.088 billion in dividends, while FCF was $2.931 billion. Despite the decline from $3.32 billion, HPQ's conservative payout ratio gives them flexibility to pursue growth and share repurchases.

Shareholder returns have dwindled in the past 5 years. In 2021, HPQ returned a total of $7.2 billion. In 2025, they returned approximately $1.9 billion through dividends and buybacks.

In Q1, free cash flow was $200 million, and $600 million was returned to shareholders. This past November, management raised the dividend 4% to $0.30 a share.

While dividend growth will likely be slow, their free cash flow profile gives them room for future increases and makes them worthy if looking for income.

Can HPQ See Sustainable Growth?

Likely the key question investors have seeing the low valuation on HPQ is, can they see growth ahead? So far, management seems to be on the right track. But as previously mentioned, investors shouldn't expect any major growth for at least a year.

And the company plans to leverage AI to drive growth by increasing software development, improving customer services & support, and driving productivity. Last month, they introduced a series of new devices aimed at streamlining operations and increasing workflow.

This includes new printers & PCs. Furthermore, the company has been collaborating with behemoths like Intel (INTC) and Autodesk (ADSK) and announced during earnings that they started a partnership with OpenAI to pilot a new enterprise platform.

So, HPQ seems to be doing all the right things. But the question is, can these drive growth, leading to share price expansion in the future? Analysts do expect growth to turn positive in 2027, with meaningful growth coming in 2028.

Forward P/E 6.88x Attractive

Using management's guidance midpoint of $3.05, HPQ's forward multiple could be very attractive at less than 7.0x.

As I mentioned earlier, if you're less risk-averse and see light at the end of the tunnel for HPQ, then buying here could pay off big long-term.

Using FastGraphs' normal multiple of 9.84x, HPQ offers solid upside to their price target of $32.50 by the end of 2028. For comparison, peer Dell Technologies (DELL) trades well above with a forward P/E of 16.36x.

Risks and Conclusion

Going forward, I do believe HPQ is susceptible to more downside risks. The main element that will drive growth and allow them to see share price expansion is if they can successfully leverage artificial intelligence to drive sustainable growth.

I do expect volatility and believe investors should keep a close eye on segment progress in the coming quarters. If Printing continues to see margin pressures with no clear improvements, the stock could continue to trend lower.

During Q1, management stated that rising input costs, driven by increasing prices in DRAM and NAND, are likely to continue in FY'26 and possibly into 2027.

Cost-saving initiatives are in effect. But the murky macro environment will also play a role in the turnaround progress.

While the low multiple and dividend yield above 6% are incentives to invest, I would like to see sustained progress, particularly in their Printing segment before considering upgrading them to a buy.

With this unlikely to come to fruition for at least another year, I'm initiating a hold rating despite the attractive yield and low valuation.

data.title: Leonardo: This European Defense Giant Is Quietly Fixing Its Biggest Weakness
data.description:
Shares of European aerospace and defense giant Leonardo S.p.a. (FINMY)(FINMF) has gained 3.7% since my last report. The stock reached a 52-week high of $75.88, which was less than $2 below my price target. So, I saw around 32% upside, and Leonardo only reached 29% before the stock headed down and now underperforms the broader market. It is a painful reminder that sometimes market outperformance fades as we wait for the price target to be reached. In this report, I discuss the company’s earnings for 2025, the outlook for 2026, and an update to my price target.

Leonardo Exceeded Guidance In 2025

Leonardo had an extremely strong year; the company’s revenues grew 10.9% year-on-year to €18.6 billion, while EBITA rose 18.2% to €1.75 billion, with margins expanding 60 basis points to 9%. Free cash flow grew 20.5% to €1 billion, while net debt improved by 44.2% to €1.1 billion. Not only did the company show strong year-on-year growth, but it also exceeded its guidance substantially. Orders grew 14.5% to €23.8 billion, indicating a 1.2x book-to-bill ratio.

In the helicopter business, order intake increased 5.1% to €6.17 billion, while revenues grew 11.1% to €5.83 billion, indicating a 1.1x book-to-bill ratio. Revenues were driven by the AW-family helicopter deliveries shifting towards higher-value units, offsetting the lower units delivered, and growing customer support services. EBITA rose 12.5% to €523 million, indicating a slight margin expansion of 10 basis points to 9.0%. The helicopter business faced some tariff pressures on helicopters destined for the US civil market, but total non-domestic sales for Leonardo as a whole are just 5%.

The aircraft division saw revenues increase 13.4% to €3.59 billion, while EBITA rose 7.2% to €460 million. Despite the solid sales growth, the EBITA margin contracted from 13.6% to 12.8%, driven by mix effects. One program with dilutive margins was the Global Combat Air Program, or GCAP, where Leonardo’s Aircraft division acts as prime contractor, creating some standalone margin dilution even though other Leonardo divisions also contribute to the program. While margins declined year-on-year, the aircraft business profile remains solid, and the division continues to stand out with EBITA margins close to 13%.

The Aerostructures business remains loss-making. EBITA improved 20.2% to a €134 million loss on stable revenues of €745 million. Order inflow increased 31.4% to €909 million, supported by higher activity, particularly on the Boeing 787 program. Loss margins improved to -18.0% from -22.5% a year earlier, indicating some operational progress but still reflecting a structurally challenged business.

The improvement was partly supported by higher production rates on the Boeing 787, which increased from 4 to 7 shipsets per month during the year, as well as some one-off benefits in Q4, including contingency releases, which drove margin performance for the quarter to break even. However, that is not a run rate for Leonardo’s aerostructures business.

The main challenge is that higher volumes alone are not yet sufficient to restore profitability, as the cost structure still needs further optimization. The aerostructures business is essentially scaled substantially higher than the volumes they are now processing, but even when the existing business scales back, the performance is unlikely to be impressive. For the current scale of the business to make sense, existing programs need to go up in production rates, and new initiatives, such as the expansion of military aerostructures and additional work on commercial programs, need to be considered.

In recent years, Leonardo started to scale its cyber operations, and by 2024, it became a standalone segment, reflecting its importance as one of Leonardo’s growth pillars. We are seeing Cyber now as the company’s segment with the highest growth in revenues.

For FY2025, revenues increased 23.1% to €798 million, while EBITA surged 63.3% to €80 million. This indicates a margin expansion from 7.6% to 10.0%, reflecting strong operating leverage as scale improves and fixed costs are absorbed more efficiently. Order intake grew 26.3% to €1.05 billion, implying a healthy book-to-bill ratio of roughly 1.3x and supporting continued growth.

Whereas the cyber business provides the highest growth in sales, the highest EBITA growth segment is the space segment. Revenues increased 11.1% to €1.01 billion, while EBITA surged 90.3% to €59 million, indicating strong operating leverage and recovery with margins expanding from 3.4% to 5.9%. The improvement is driven both by strong execution in the services segment and a reduction in losses from Thales Alenia Space, which remains loss-making but is progressing in its recovery plan.

Order intake rose 9.4% to €1.05 billion, confirming continued solid demand in the space domain. European space companies and segments have faced pressure from competition and cost inflation in recent years, but the improving profitability and reduced losses from joint ventures suggest that the segment is moving toward a more sustainable earnings contribution.

The defense electronics business showed strong growth, but with some divergence between Europe and the U.S. operations. In Europe, revenues increased 11.4% to €5.14 billion, while EBITA rose 10.1% to €738 million. Margins were down 20 basis points to 14.3%, reflecting mix effects and the impact from joint ventures, despite an underlying improvement in Electronics Europe's profitability. Orders grew 7.8% to €6.92 billion, implying a book-to-bill ratio of around 1.3x.

In the U.S. business, Leonardo DRS delivered revenues of $3.65 billion, up 12.8%, while EBITA increased 17.2% to $381 million. Margins expanded 40 basis points to 10.4%, reflecting improved profitability and operating leverage. Orders rose 4.1% to $4.25 billion, corresponding to a book-to-bill ratio of roughly 1.2x.

So, we see strong revenues and orders for both segments, but the U.S. business is showing better margins and margin expansion compared to the European business, including joint ventures. Excluding joint ventures, Electronics Europe improved margins from 10.3% to close to 11%. So, there is no operational weakness, but the after-tax contributions of joint ventures did affect the margins for the business, including joint ventures.

Leonardo Targets For 2026 Are Embedded In Multi-Year Framework With Strong Backlog Visibility

The guidance for 2026 is embedded in the company’s industrial plan. The industrial plan targets €30 billion in revenues by 2030, EBITA of €3.6 billion, indicating margins of 12%, and free cash flow of around €2.1 billion. For 2026, the company targets €21 billion in sales and EBITA of €2.03 billion, indicating margins of 9.7% compared to 9% in 2025. It is interesting to note that the company expects around €142 billion in orders through 2030, which means that the company’s backlog will grow from €46.6 billion in 2025 to €62.5 billion in 2030. Leonardo is not aggressive on its revenue ramp schedule, with, for instance, mid-teens growth, but it is targeting consistent high single-digit growth with a backlog that covers roughly 2 years of sales.

Leonardo Stock Remains A Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Leonardo, I used to set the price target based on the average of the company’s 10-year EV/EBITDA-based price target and the peer group's implied price target. I have now changed that to using the company’s 3-year EV/EBITDA median, and that yields a $70.57 price target, implying 15% upside. It also provides some reasoning on why the stock did not hit my previous price target. It was simply too loosely based on taking the price target between the peer group valuation and the 10-year EV/EBITDA for the company. Likewise, it is unfortunate, because if I had used the 3-year EV/EBITDA in my prior assessment as well, the price target would have been reached and the call would have worked out. So, it not only shows the importance of analyzing earnings but also of using better-suited valuation frameworks and multiples.

For Leonardo, the EBITDA margins are expected to expand. It is not a huge jump, with 30 basis points expected this year, another 50 the year after, and another 30 basis points in 2028, but over time it does add up. Especially for a company as big as Leonardo, which has grown into a rather inefficient defense contractor, seeing a focus on margin expansion is good. The company’s free cash flow is expected to show an inflection in 2027 while capital expenditures remain around 5% of revenues. The company is expected to enter a net cash position by 2027. So, what I would like to see is some acquisition activity to put the money to good use or for Leonardo to actually put together a share repurchase program.

Conclusion: Leonardo Still Looks Attractive

I believe that Leonardo is finally gaining momentum as a business. Not just by growing sales, but also by turning its business more profitable by cutting excess costs. That should position the company to grow its sales more efficiently amid a defense spending surge globally and particularly in Europe as part of the initiative for security autonomy. Leonardo is well-positioned for that with multi-domain presence, including cyber as a growth pillar, and joint ventures giving the company a reach that extends beyond its own business segments.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Janus Henderson Balanced Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund (JDBAX) returned -4.90% and the S&P 500® Index returned -4.33%. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returned -0.05%. The Balanced Index returned -2.62%.

Contributors/detractors

Asset allocation positioning and overall security selection detracted from relative performance. Stock selection in financials and overweight allocations to loans and securitized credit helped offset underperformance.

Outlook

Despite near-term geopolitical uncertainty, we believe substantial technology infrastructure investment and artificial intelligence (AI)-driven productivity gains create a favorable economic backdrop.

Portfolio management

Jeremiah Buckley, CFA

Greg Wilensky, CFA

Michael Keough

Investment environment

  • In equities, the market declined as the Iran conflict sparked volatility and pushed oil prices higher. This created concerns about downstream inflationary impacts on consumers and overall spending patterns, particularly in travel-related industries.
  • Early in the quarter, investors grew more cautious about the valuations and capital spending of large U.S. technology companies. Software stocks, in particular, saw multiple compression due to fears of AI-related disruption. Against this backdrop, investors rotated away from growth stocks toward value-oriented and defensive sectors.
  • In fixed income, markets moved from early-year optimism around declining inflation and prospective rate cuts to renewed concerns about inflation and geopolitical risk. Treasuries and securitized sectors outperformed investment-grade (IG) and high-yield corporates as investors priced in the impacts of higher geopolitical risk.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) kept the fed funds rate unchanged at 3.50% to 3.75%, while noting the “uncertain” implications of the Middle East conflict. Having previously discounted two or three more cuts in 2026, futures markets now are anticipating no change in Fed policy.
  • The U.S. 10-year Treasury yield ended the quarter 15 basis points (bps) higher, at 4.32%. The yield curve flattened out meaningfully as 2-year Treasury yields rose more than 10-year yields when markets repriced rate-cut expectations.

Portfolio review

We entered the quarter with roughly 64.5% in equities and 35.5% in fixed income and the portfolio closed March with approximately 64.2% in equities and 35.8% in fixed income. The overweight to equities and the corresponding underweight to fixed income versus the Balanced Index hurt relative performance as equities trailed bonds.

In equities, the energy and information technology sectors detracted from relative performance, while financials and consumer discretionary contributed.

In terms of individual stock performance, Lam Research was among the top relative contributors. The semiconductor equipment maker benefited from continued demand for graphics processing units (GPUs) and central processing units (CPUs), which is driving the need for additional semiconductor capacity. Lam’s strong position in memory production equipment – a critical bottleneck in the AI infrastructure buildout – also helped drive performance. The company maintains a solid balance sheet and consistently has returned capital to shareholders, reinforcing our conviction in its long-term value.

Chevron also was among the top relative contributors, benefiting from the sharp rise in oil prices driven by the Iran war. The integrated energy company may be well positioned to capitalize on elevated crude prices as its U.S.-based production assets could be less affected by Middle East disruptions. In addition, operational improvements have supported a recovery in the company's valuation multiple.

Microsoft Corporation was among the top relative detractors. While earnings estimates remained stable, the enterprise software and cloud infrastructure company faced pressure from two broad market concerns: First, acceleration in capital expenditures (capex) by hyperscalers raised questions about long-term profit margins, although we believe management will try to offset some of the impact through AI-driven productivity improvements and slower operating expense growth. Second, as a major application software provider, Microsoft faced concerns about AI disruption to its software businesses.

American Express also detracted from relative performance. The payment services and credit card company faced multiple headwinds during the quarter. Market concerns around the impact of the Iran conflict weighed on the stock, particularly given its significant exposure to travel services. Higher energy prices raised fears that travel could become more expensive for both consumers and businesses, potentially dampening demand. Additionally, regulatory concerns around credit-card interest rates and broader fears of AI disruption to high return on invested capital (ROIC) businesses pressured the stock.

Portfolio activity within the equity portion of the portfolio was elevated amid market volatility. We reduced exposure to software and financials, reallocating to industrials, materials, and utilities. We also modestly increased energy holdings in response to higher oil prices. These changes were aimed at maintaining the equity portfolio's overall risk profile while potentially improving positioning for current market dynamics.

In fixed income, yield curve positioning was the primary detractor for the quarter. We were overweight the front end of the yield curve, which hurt on a relative basis as short-term yields rose significantly and the curve flattened. Our overweight to credit spread risk also detracted as spreads broadly widened amid conflict in the Middle East. Concerns surrounding AI disruption was another key source of spread widening, as software and services sectors sold off.

Asset allocation decisions contributed, with overweight allocations to loans and securitized credit helping relative returns. Within our securitized exposure, allocations to private-label collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs), commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), and asset-backed securities (ABS) fared well on a relative basis.

During the quarter, we reduced our exposure to Treasuries in favor of an increased allocation to corporate credit, as we took advantage of improved entry points due to spread widening. We also increased our allocation to agency MBS as spreads widened amid elevated rate volatility.

Manager outlook

Despite the potential economic impacts from the Iran conflict, we maintain an optimistic outlook on continued economic growth, supported by a robust capital spending environment, ongoing productivity gains, and strong consumer fundamentals.

The Iran conflict is the most significant new development this year. While we do not expect a prolonged disruption, oil prices are averaging higher and could pressure consumer spending and inflation. Tax refunds and lower withholding may help offset some of this pressure. Prior to the conflict, consumer spending already was stronger than expected, with solid credit card activity and robust travel demand. While higher energy prices have tempered some of the potential upside in our outlook, the underlying strength in consumer balance sheets and spending patterns remains intact.

On the capital spending side, AI infrastructure investment – particularly in data center buildouts – continues to exceed expectations and drive meaningful economic impact. The memory bottleneck that previously constrained progress is easing as supply responds to demand, benefiting semiconductor capital equipment companies. Industrial capital expenditures tied to data center construction, including electrical equipment, power systems, and HVAC, also have remained strong. Given the importance of AI-infrastructure investment to the economy, we are closely monitoring to ensure that companies achieve appropriate returns and execute backlogs, and that revenue supports those backlogs.

Despite increased volatility, we have not materially changed our earnings growth assumptions. We continue to expect mid-teens earnings growth in 2026 and low-teens growth in 2027. Companies are realizing productivity gains from technology investments, which should support margins.

One source of recent market uncertainty we continue to watch is AI disruption. Software, information technology services, and financials have seen broad multiple compression, even though many of these companies have maintained stable earnings trajectories. We believe many high-quality companies will adapt by using AI to improve products and efficiency. The current phase of indiscriminate selling has created a stark divergence where growth sectors with rising earnings estimates are being penalized, while defensive sectors with declining estimates are being rewarded. We view this dislocation as excessive, though near-term relief appears unlikely.

In fixed income, short- and intermediate-term yields have moved higher, driven not only by inflation expectations but also by rising real yields. This is notable given that elevated energy prices typically weigh on economic growth. We believe front-end duration offers a better hedge in the event of further escalation in the Middle East, as higher oil prices ultimately could lead to lower rates. Front-end positioning also may benefit from a resolution to the conflict and reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Looking ahead, we expect fixed income markets to remain volatile but constructive. Elevated starting yields provide an attractive income cushion, while economic growth remains supported by AI-driven investment, strong corporate fundamentals, and lower taxes. Inflation and Fed policy will remain key drivers of market direction. In this environment, we seek to maintain diversified sources of income, actively manage duration risk, and emphasize credit quality and security selection.

As always, we will dynamically adjust each of the equity and fixed income allocations, as well as the portfolio's overall mix between equities and fixed income, as we analyze the risks and opportunities in each market.

data.title: Analyzing The Potential Sirius XM And iHeart Media Merger
data.description:
Sirius XM and iHeart Media merger rumors

Last Friday, rumors about Sirius XM (SIRI) and iHeart Media (IHRT) exploring a merger confounded investors. SIRI stock suffered a bit, while IHRT stock soared.

There were actually two different story lines: the first saw Sirius XM as the acquirer, while the second saw music mogul Irving Azoff as the one trying to acquire both companies.

Personally, I believe the second version is the least likely. Irving Azoff does have a lot of experience in the music sector, mainly as a record executive and talent manager, but he does not have any history of large-scale, multi-billion M&A transactions.

On the other hand, I would not be surprised at all if Sirius XM took a run at iHeart Media, as its chairman, Greg Maffei, in his previous role as Liberty Media CEO, has already tried to buy a major stake in iHeart’s pre-bankruptcy predecessor.

Old acquaintances

First of all, iHeart once owned a large stake in Sirius XM, which gave it the right to transmit its content via satellite radio. After it had sold its stake in 2013, its stations left Sirius XM.

A few years later, during Liberty Media’s Q4/17 conference call, Greg Maffei explained why he was trying to buy a major stake in iHeart:

I think there are potentially substantial synergies between iHeart and both Pandora and Sirius. The Sirius ones are probably more of a revenue nature, the opportunities are on cross-promotion around sharing personalities as you rightly point that about, what 360L may be able to do, to bring strength for iHeart into the car in differentiated ways and probably protect the position there, FM Radio has in the car. And on the cost side, Pandora probably has a substantial number of well, around shared add technology around leveraging, sales force around some of those things that could become as well as some revenue opportunities around those things. The sales force at iHeart is a very powerful one and larger than substantially in the ad sales force of either Pandora or Sirius and much more focused on some of the local components. So there are potentially a range of synergies and the cost side, a range of series. On the revenue side and some of them are also about the funnel of free users and how we can move some of them into some of those pay categories.

At the time, iHeart was trying to avoid bankruptcy. Liberty Media apparently was willing to value the company at $7.5 billion, while senior creditors wanted 12% more. Since there was no agreement, on March 15, 2018, iHeart eventually filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy.

Why a combination of Sirius XM and iHeart makes sense

As we have seen, until 2013 there already was substantial cooperation between the two companies. From then on they became pure competitors, and Sirius XM has always highlighted that it doesn’t see radio streaming as its key competitor but terrestrial radio.

iHeart owns north of 800 radio stations and actually is the largest radio station group owner in the U.S., both by revenue and number of stations. It reaches over 110 million listeners every week.

If the two companies merged, they would not need to compete anymore for the same talent and could offer creators much greater scale, thus reducing content acquisition costs. Sirius XM would get its hands on a huge funnel of free listeners to potentially convert into paying subscribers.

Back in 2018, potential operational synergies were estimated at $500 million.

Sirius XM is forecast to deliver $1.5 billion of free cash flow in 2027, while iHeart should end up with around $200 million. That said, iHeart is heavily burdened by about $430 million of interest payments on its huge debt of about $5 billion, which boils down to an interest rate of almost 9%.

Effectively, despite the $200 million of FCF, the equity has a total market cap of just $840 million, highlighting investors’ concerns with its indebtedness.

That said, since Sirius XM has stable cash inflows and recently refinanced debt for an interest rate below 6%, after a merger it could probably refinance iHeart’s debt at lower rates, saving up to a third of iHeart’s current interest costs, or $140 million.

Combined with operational synergies, the business combination could easily produce north of $500 million of additional free cash flow, without even taking into consideration potential revenue synergies.

If there are still tax loss carryforwards from the bankruptcy, further tax benefits would also factor into the calculation.

I reference 2027 estimates since any merger would necessarily need to wait until at least the end of 2026, given the limitations resulting from Sirius XM’s Liberty Media transaction. Moreover, the Sirius XM FCF estimate does not take into account potential spectrum lease agreements.

If Sirius XM wanted to acquire iHeart, I believe the valuation is highly unlikely to reach the $7.5B price tag offered in 2018 by Liberty Media. Considering iHeart’s high leverage and low cash generation, it is always in need of complex debt restructurings. In contrast, Liberty Media could bring to the table a lower cost of debt, stable cash flows, massive synergies, cross-selling opportunities, and robust synergies with other companies from the Liberty portfolio, such as Formula One (FWONA) (FWONK) and Live Nation (LYV). Still, an EV of $6.5 billion would still lead to an IHRT stock price of about twice the current level.

As far as Sirius XM is concerned, once merged with iHeart, it would carry $15 billion of debt (assuming a $6.2 billion price tag) and probably produce over $4 billion of EBITDA, bringing its leverage to about 3.75x. This would be worse than its current 3.38x, but very manageable, given the expected FCF of $2.2 billion (assuming a total merger benefit of $500 million on FCF and no spectrum lease benefit). Within two years after the merger, debt could be back down around 3x.

Conclusion

Some investors would probably prefer Sirius XM to stay out of large-scale M&A at this point, when the stock finally starts to move higher and the potential spectrum lease agreements on the horizon provide some nice near-term excitement. Yet the long-term benefits from an iHeart merger could even be greater than those from spectrum leasing.

Given that the current administration seems likely to wave through legacy media mergers (see Paramount Skydance (PSKY) and Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD)), there is a good chance it will also allow greater concentration in the radio space. Hence, the opportunity is now and must be caught quickly.

As a result, there is very likely a lot of truth behind the swirling M&A rumors. Investors will obviously need to assess the details if and when any deal will be agreed.

As far as Sirius XM is concerned, neither its major owner, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), nor its chairman Greg Maffei, is known for doing dumb deals, so I trust that they will walk away if they don’t get a great deal.

As far as iHeart is concerned, it is hard to understand who effectively is in charge, as not much is known about the owners of its debt. Stock appreciation is not their key concern anyway, as they might not even own the equity. This is why I will stay away from speculating on a quick profit on IHRT stock.

data.title: CIO Weekly: Japan Shows Resilience
data.description:
By Joe Amato

Japan's energy import dependence makes it one of the conflict's more exposed markets. Yet its short-term resilience is high, and the longer-term investment case remains attractive.

Many of Asia's major economies rank among the most vulnerable to the energy shock from the Middle East conflict, a reality reflected in the sharp equity market sell-offs across the region when the war began in late February.

While most indices have since recovered on fundamentals and signs of de-escalation, my recent business trip to Japan and Korea made clear that the region remains on edge. Uncertainty around the conflict's impact on energy supply has not dissipated.

Japan's exposure is structural. Crude oil accounts for around 30% of the country's total energy consumption, with approximately 90% of imports sourced from the Middle East—principally Saudi Arabia and the UAE. Japan is also one of the largest LNG importers among developed economies, though its LNG supply chain is more geographically diversified, offering a partial offset to its concentrated crude oil dependency.

Resilient Factors

The near- and medium-term impact of the conflict on energy supply will have weighed on the Bank of Japan's deliberations at its meeting this week, where it is expected to leave interest rates unchanged, preserving optionality ahead of June as the inflationary effects of the energy shock become clearer.

What has helped is that oil prices have retreated from their early April highs, supported in part by flows through the Saudi East-West pipeline, which has prevented prices from reaching more alarming levels. Our base case remains that demand destruction does not materialize until oil sustains $150 – $175 a barrel for six months or more. At the time of writing, oil was trading in the mid-$90s.

Beyond the oil price, Japan's macro buffers have provided meaningful insulation. The country holds approximately 250 days of strategic oil reserves—one of the largest buffers in Asia and among developed economies—and the government moved swiftly to reinstate gasoline subsidies, cushioning the consumer impact and limiting the scale of the equity sell-off relative to regional peers.

Constructively Positioned

While Japan remains one of the most exposed developed economies to the conflict, we remain constructive and our overweight in Japanese equities is unchanged. My recent visit reinforced rather than challenged that view.

The long-term investment case rests on several important factors. Corporate governance reform continues to gain traction, with a deepening focus on shareholder value driving a structural improvement in return profiles. The economy is strengthening on stabilizing inflation and meaningful gains in real wage growth, while companies with pricing power are able to pass on higher input costs to protect margins. Reinforced by a resounding election victory, Prime Minister Takaichi's economic and fiscal policies are broadly supportive, particularly in strategic sectors including AI, defense and shipbuilding.

The snap election in February delivered a decisive majority, reducing policy uncertainty and providing the kind of political continuity that has historically been a tailwind for Japanese risk assets—most notably during the Koizumi and Abe periods. The Nikkei 225 index is up about 18% in dollar terms year-to-date and around the same in local currency, making it one of the best-performing major markets this year.

Long-Term Attraction

The risks to this outlook are, however, very real. The Middle East conflict and its effect on global energy prices remain the primary concern. Yen volatility is another. And a less discussed but potentially significant risk lies in supply constraints on industrial materials—sulfur, helium and related inputs critical to manufacturing processes. Should disruption extend into the summer, Japanese industrial production could face capacity constraints with knock-on consequences for global supply chains.

That said, the foundations underpinning our constructive view are durable. A stable government, substantial strategic reserves, renewed fiscal support, recovering real wages, and a corporate earnings cycle that remains structurally positive provide a platform for recovery as the macro environment stabilizes.

Beyond Japan, the vested interests pushing toward de-escalation in the Middle East are substantial, spanning energy importers, exporters and major consumers alike. Our base case remains that the Strait situation resolves. The range of outcomes is wide, but absent a breakdown in negotiations or a sharp move higher in oil, we remain constructive on Japan and on global growth and risk assets more broadly.

  • U.S. CB Consumer Confidence
