data.title: MaxLinear: Re-Rated Too Far, Too Fast
data.description:
MaxLinear’s (MXL) latest earnings came out on April 23, and the stock saw a huge ~80% move right away. The reaction isn’t just due to the quarterly beat, even if that was part of the story. A second part is that the company is moving from being a traditional broadband and connectivity chip vendor to a participant in AI-driven data center infrastructure. These two coincided with a strong move in the market surrounding AI names—and the stock was suddenly, heavily rerated. While the underlying narrative is real, the magnitude of the multiple expansion suggests much of the near-term upside should already be priced in. I will therefore argue for a Hold rating at current levels with a modest possibility of downside unless execution exceeds expectations.

Moving on, MaxLinear is a fabless semiconductor company. It mainly designs communications SoCs in broadband, connectivity, and infrastructure markets. Earlier, its revenue mainly came from cable modems, Wi-Fi gateways, and networking equipment—cyclical, lower-multiple businesses.

The recent change is MaxLinear’s shifting of focus into optical data center interconnect, particularly through its PAM4 digital signal processing (DSP) platforms used in 400G and 800G optical modules. These are increasingly critical components of hyperscale data centers. Those need higher bandwidth and interconnect density. MaxLinear is in just the right place at the right time.

This is an important shift because MaxLinear has moved from a lower-multiple segment of the semiconductor industry to one that is higher-growth and comes with higher multiples. Management is pointing to accelerating demand and a “step-function” increase in data center-related revenue. Along with compute and memory, optical interconnect is a key enabler of AI infrastructure scaling, so companies that become a part of this buildout see significant valuation expansion.

My concern, however, is whether the market has already priced MaxLinear for a completely successful execution of this transition.

Q1 Earnings

Q1 results this year were markedly strong.

Not only was this a beat-and-raise quarter, it also showed a shift in where the revenue was coming from:

Note the marked improvement in infrastructure revenue, up 136% YoY. Led by optical data center products, this is now the largest segment and primary driver of growth. Management said this was the start of a “multiyear growth phase,” noting they expect a “step-function” increase in data center revenue starting in Q2. That means they expect a discrete jump next quarter—this is an important idea to note.

MaxLinear’s forward visibility is improving, with the 2026 optical data center outlook now at $150-170 million. This is driven by stronger order flow and more business from hyperscalers. If I were to point to one single item for the re-rating, it is this forward visibility, not just the Q1 beat.

However, the company is still not profitable:

They may have improved their topline, but it has not yet trickled down to their bottom line. They are still GAAP loss-making, and their operating cash flow remains negative. Continued investments as well as stock-based compensations drive operating expenses higher.

The takeaway from Q1 earnings is the visible demand acceleration in AI-linked optical business. This is not another AI hype. There is real growth here, even if the current results still reflect a company in transition.

Why the Stock Moved

I have noted that three drivers, not earnings beat alone, moved the stock so much yesterday. These are listed below:

The first driver is earnings, which explains why the stock moved up at all. The earnings beat and the guidance shift made MXL a noticeable name in the market. This gave it the initial impetus.

The second driver is narrative shift. This explains why it moved so much. The high increase in infrastructure mix helped re-rate the stock.

The third driver is a sector-wide tailwind. This explains why the move came so quickly and was so broad-based. Many semiconductor names moved up, led by MXL, with Intel (INTC) up ~24%, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) ~13%, and so on. This reflected a broad-based strength in AI names.

Lastly, I should also mention positioning. MXL was not widely recognized as an AI-related name. However, the narrative changed—with proof—right on the day AI stocks were moving up. MXL just caught the drift full on its sails and went up ~80%.

Whether such a move is sustainable or not depends on the fundamentals holding up the sail—i.e., valuation.

Valuation—Pricing In Success Already

Table: Current Baseline

MXL is trading at ~5.5x forward sales, which is already at premium levels compared to the sector median of roughly ~3.0x. This indicates a re-rating due to a shift towards AI multiples, not just MXL’s slow-growth legacy communications valuation.

Table: Scenario Analysis

Interpretation

I have ranked the three scenarios and made some revenue and multiple assumptions.

In the bear case scenario, narrative has moved ahead of fundamentals, so there’s a correction. Optical segment ramp is slower than anticipated here, with hyperscaler demand not scaling quickly enough. Q1 revenue was ~$550 million annualized, and in my bear case, revenue settles closer to ~$575 million—not much improvement, in contrast to management's expectation of a step-up in revenue—so the market goes back to valuing MXL as another communications semiconductor with no direct relation to AI. So sector median multiple sets in, and we get a meaningful downside of up to 50%. This does not require a collapse in the business—as we do see, revenue has actually improved here. We only need normalized growth and unlinking from AI. That is enough to collapse the high valuation of today. However, to be clear, this is not a realistic scenario given management guidance.

The base case assumes a roughly ~15% revenue improvement, driven by optical, but the follow-through is moderate. That compresses multiples a little—given disappointment surrounding management’s step-up language—making the stock fully valued.

One important nuance I must clarify here. In their earnings call, management noted a step-up in optical revenue: “We also expect a step function data center revenue increase beginning in Q2 with expected strong upside as run rates expand into 2027.” Now, normally, a step up is a whole number multiple in my book—a 2x or 3x ramp-up, to be precise. However, when questioned by analysts, management declined to quantify what the step-up would be. Indeed, the analyst from Northland Capital Markets was much more conservative: “Given the step-up we're seeing in Q2, do you have any comments about overall revenue growth expectations for '26? It looks like we could be tracking, I don't know, 35%, 40%, but any comment from the company?”

In my bull case scenario, I have assumed a ~32% revenue improvement, sticking to just below the lower number in that range. My bull case assumes a decently strong multiple expansion over the revenue ramp-up. Together, the revenue and the multiple expansion give us a near-50% upside—assuming the company can credibly execute and monetize its AI infrastructure. This is meaningful upside, not extreme. If we were to take the step-up language too literally, the upside would be extreme.

Positioning

Currently, the stock sits between the base and bull case in terms of upside, but given the revenue expansion setup, it is closer to pricing in success. That’s the asymmetry - upside depends on success, but downside doesn’t need complete failure. Upside needs continued outperformance; downside only needs normalization. This simply means that valuation is now driven by confidence, not by fundamentals—nothing wrong in that, except that makes the stock highly sensitive to execution.

Conclusion

MaxLinear’s results easily demonstrate the transition happening at the company. It is strongly linking itself to the AI momentum through its optical interconnect (particularly Keystone, the PAM4 DSP platform) offering, improving revenue visibility. The market has responded by re-rating the stock much above the sector average, leaning near AI-level multiples. So the issue isn’t how the business is going, but the price new investors must pay for that re-rating.

As I showed in the valuation, current levels already reflect a successful ramp in optical data center revenue. Upside now depends on continued positive surprises, while downside only needs a little bit of below-expected delivery. This creates an unfavorable risk-reward asymmetry in the near term.

This is why I rate MXL a Hold, with a downside bias. I clearly acknowledge the improved fundamentals, but I also recognize that valuation has moved way ahead of what is fully proven.

The key risk, therefore, is execution. The way this is being valued today assumes the optical ramp comes through fully. Any delay—quite typical in the industry—could cause a multiple compression. Upside only comes through surprises. Hold.

data.title: General Motors: Structural Transformation Underway And Undervalued (Q1 Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Introduction

Automakers belong to an industry that has, as a whole, meaningfully underperformed the market. Yet, every now and then, we find a real outperformer. After the pandemic, it was Stellantis (STLA), but in 2024, the baton was taken by General Motors (GM). Since my last article on GM (written in October 2025), the stock is up by over 34%, beating the market's 7.5% return over the same period of time. Many things have been said about the company. In particular, its CEO, Mary Barra's efforts to reduce production complexity, thus reducing manufacturing costs while keeping production quality at good levels, have been repeatedly highlighted. In this article, however, I would like to point out that, as GM's earnings approach, we should start changing the way we view the overall business.

In fact, we usually look at auto demand, which is currently solid, or we look at wholesale used car prices as another important metric about the car industry's health. While auto manufacturing is surely a business that requires volumes, there are two particular developments that we should be aware of. The first one is that, in a world where geo-capex matters, automakers can play a role in military production. It is not surprising to read that the Pentagon held talks with GM and Ford on this matter. As a result, these companies could see some favorable multiple expansion in case they develop a meaningful defense business. Secondly, and this will be more important for this earnings preview, GM is developing a profitable recurring revenue business.

GM's Q1 2026 Earnings Preview

Let's start with some numbers, so that we can better understand what the new transformation of GM could look like. Investors believe that GM will report $43.68B in revenues (-0.8% YoY) and EPS of $2.62 (-5.8% YoY). These declines won't appear as surprises, since the guidance GM issued in January when it released its Q4 2025 earnings was clear. For the fiscal year, GM said it was expecting North America margins to improve and be back to the 8%-10% range, with industrial FCF of $10B and EPS of $12. So, as the year goes by, GM should be able to pick up momentum and strength.

We already know the US and China Q1 sales releases. In the domestic market, sales were down by almost 10% because of tough comparables. However, truck demand is strong, and this is important for GM's profitability. If you remember, people bought cars last March in anticipation of tariffs. In China, things keep being challenging, with sales down 22% YoY to 350k units. So, given these reports, I think we might end up seeing GM come a bit below its guidance.

We know that tariffs will have a $750M-$1B impact on GM, and this is enough to explain why we are expecting EPS to be down by around 6% YoY: this is the last quarter that is compared to a no-tariff environment. Now, we need to wait a bit before we know how the new tariff environment will be. So, it is hard to make predictions for Q2 and Q3 to this extent.

However, we also know that GM has achieved profitability in lower-tier vehicles, a category that previously represented a bloodbath for the company. As we heard at the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago's Automotive Insights Symposium last year, GM "sold over 700,000 vehicles priced under $30,000" at a profit.

But the real aspect that is changing GM is what the company described in detail at the Bank of America Global Automotive Summit. Here, GM's CFO Paul Jacobson explained the economics of the connected services business that GM has built in the past few years, and that is going to be a game-changer for the company's earnings. GM has two products that are bundled together as connectivity services: OnStar (a safety and connectivity platform) and SuperCruise (the hands-free driver assistance system). As of FY25, the two services combined reported a recognized revenue of $2.7B, while we see that $5.4B was recognized as deferred revenue in the balance sheet. GM reported 12M subscribers (620k for SuperCruise, up 80% YoY), with a gross margin of 70%.

What matters even more is the strategy. Once we understand it, we can better grasp why GM is probably not reflecting this important shift. Once GM sells its cars, it now bundles an OnStar 8-year subscription at no additional cost. GM then allocated a bit of the vehicle sale price to the OnStar service component, which has to be recorded as deferred revenue. In FY26, the projected deferred revenue is $7.5B with $3B in recognition. If we backsolve, we see that the company is actually collecting $5.5B, while only recognizing $3B. This explains why the company's EBIT is structurally lower than the company's operating cash flow. As a result, the EBIT multiple might be underestimating the company's value.

Second aspect. GM cited at the BofA summit that it currently has around 45M vehicles in the U.S. alone. Right now, its subscribers are expected to increase to 13M by year-end, which means that the penetration rate is just below 30%. Since GM only started last year to include the 8-year OnStar basic subscription with its vehicles, every year that goes by will see older vehicles dismissed and new vehicles replacing them with the subscription bundled in. This is important because GM management disclosed that around 50% of its customers upgrade the basic subscription to premium packages, with 25% of upgraders selecting the Protect tier and another 25% of upgraders asking for the Connect Plus tier.

SuperCruise has a renewal rate between 30% and 40% after the 3-year subscription expires. Since the estimated ARPU for this service is around $25/month and there are around 250k vehicles that will hit the renewal wall by the end of 2027, we have renewals that could generate between $75M and $90M in incremental annual revenue, even without considering subscriber growth. Now, expand this over the entire business, and you can understand why GM's management was explicit: the service model revenue will dwarf the wholesale business.

So when you start to look at the ability to collect revenue and to continue to collect revenue years after the original wholesale, you can see where the revenue model actually starts to fundamentally transform because all -- as we know, these software-like margins that are coming in, in the connected business can actually drive and potentially over time, dwarf even the wholesale business, which is remarkably strong and remarkably large. So we're seeing really positive early results. And this is even before we get to software-defined vehicles, as we've talked about in 2028, where the number of services, the number of applications, and the number of ways to interact with our customers is even greater than what it is today.

We should also be aware that GM has started testing next-generation autonomous driving and that, beginning in 2028, the Cadillac Escalade IQ should introduce an eyes-off self-driving system. If GM maintains this promise, we can bet that Super Cruise subscriptions will grow.

Valuation

What I tried to show explains why, this time, I want to value GM using an SOTP method because we need to understand that the company now has three revenue streams: automotive operations, connected services, and the financing branch.

Let's start with the automotive branch, which should report FY26 EBIT of 10.5B. We can use a multiple of 5.5x, and we have an EV of $57.75B.

Let's now turn to connected services. Considering its incredibly high gross margin and its expected growth, we could expect its EBIT to be at least $1B at the end of this year. Here, we have to use a higher multiple. I will use 15x, even though I think I am being conservative. This leads to an EV of $15B. So auto and services reach an EV of $72.75B.

GM Financial should report FY26 EBIT flat YoY, so I will consider $2B in net income and use an equity multiple of 10 (I am considering that GM carries almost $100B in financing debt, so an EV here can be misleading). The equity value is $20B.

The outcome is that GM's equity value is $92.75. But its market cap is currently $70.4B. This is a 31.7% upside, which doesn't even factor in, except for the 15x multiple, the fact that the connectivity business could grow faster than anticipated. Basically, we are buying GM today as if this business didn't exist. So, we are buying GM's best segment for free. This is why I am not afraid of the earnings call. It could make the stock drop a bit if deliveries are below expectations, but this would only widen the gap between the current price and its fair value, just above $100. As a result, my bull rating is intact and is actually strengthened.

data.title: Micron: The Cycle Is Evolving, Not Disappearing
data.description:
Thesis

Many analysts have already spoken on the boom-bust cycles that have plagued Micron (MU) and the semiconductor industry as a whole during the last 10 years. And, as of late, mounting evidence has surmised that this era may be over as we step into a period of extraordinary growth in the AI sector, and sustained growth at that. However, the reality is likely more nuanced than previously thought. The up/down cycles are, in fact, evolving, not completely disappearing, as AI-driven demand has raised the floor but not eliminated cyclicality in its entirety. Along with this, I see higher through-cycle margins justifying a structural re-rate that others have talked about before, and I surmise that current supply-demand constraints are driven by sustainable and rational complexity, making for longer upcycles into the future. Due to the reasons above, I am a Buy on $MU with ~50-55% upside as I see this phenomenon being priced in.

Company Background

I like to begin with a background of the company, and although I'm sure most readers already know how Micron operates, I believe it is important to provide context on the different segments that I will be speaking about later in the article. With that being said, Micron develops computer memory/storage technologies and primarily operates within two differentiated segments:

DRAM Memory (Random-Access/Fast Memory): Designs, develops, and sells DRAM memory to be used on cloud servers, PCs, and consumer electronics.

NAND Memory (Flash Storage/SSDs): Sells SSDs (Solid State Drives, NAND is a primary component) and other physical, long-term memory devices to be used in similar electronics as were stated above.

Revenues are split on a roughly 80/20 basis (2025 10-K Page 8 and 9), those being the DRAM and NAND segments, respectively. Both of these segments/memory products are critical to the rapidly growing AI infrastructure, where most of Micron's revenue lies. AI models require immense bandwidth, storage, and network capabilities to constantly train, intake, and output data, which is exactly where Micron products (HBM) come in. Beyond that, they sell typical consumer DRAM and NAND devices, used commonly in PCs and otherwise.

AI Demand Is Raising The Earnings Floor, Even In A Cyclical Market

There is no doubt that cyclical demand is principal to the semiconductor space, as we have seen in the boom-bust cycles that dominated prior years across the industry. But where is this phenomenon coming from? There is substantial evidence pointing to the fact that this cycle is a product of cyclical consumer end markets, rather than the newfound AI demand that has been seen in the past few years. PCs, smartphones, and all manner of electronics are driven by upgrade cycles and eventual market saturation.

As you can see from the graph above, consumer electronics markets are inherently volatile, and those same electronics often contain the memory that makes up a large part of Micron's revenue, making their own streams volatile at the same time. AI-driven spending contains less volatility when considered similarly. Most AI/data center spending is nondiscretionary for the most part, as spending is driven by compute requirements and necessary model scaling rather than the optional upgrades/add-ons that make up the consumer market.

In this way, consumer markets have created the volatility that often accompanies these cycles, not the underlying supply dynamics that are core to both markets. Of course, there will still be cycles, but no longer will we see the volatility that we have seen as spending shifts towards AI.

In just 5 years, Micron has seen an enormous mix shift in revenue as AI/enterprise-related revenue has increased in share by roughly ~25-30% (similar to that of Nvidia/AMD and their consumer segments) as AI spending has similarly skyrocketed and will likely continue to rise. This brings with it the decrease in volatility that will now lead to an elevated earnings floor when it comes to future cycles. The cyclical dynamic of the semi market is core to the market itself and will certainly continue to dominate market sentiment, but we will no longer see the extreme price swings in memory and thus large changes in revenue that we've seen in Micron's past, as non-discretionary spending takes a larger and larger part in revenue.

Above is an illustration of what I believe is likely to occur. The rise in AI spending is not slowing down, with projections for nearly $7 trillion in total AI-related spending by 2030, from $1.7 trillion in 2025. Micron, and the semiconductor market as a whole, is likely to benefit from this, as I see a large decrease in the current volatility of revenue, sustaining current elevated growth that will be important for the valuation that I will get into later.

Supply is Structurally, Rationally, and Beneficially Constrained

The memory industry, as technology advances and becomes more complex, is increasingly unable to meet demand as supply growth is constrained. Micron has sold out of its 2026 HBM inventory and is currently in talks with clients for 2027 inventory agreements at the same time. This comes with the advent of AI, and the phenomena that I have already spoken of earlier. Many may think this is a bearish signal, as it often indicates lost revenue or inefficient preparation/use of assets, but I argue this is another core, and advantageous, component of the industry. Supply often lags demand due to the high costs of new fabrication facilities (~$10-20 billion), something that is showcased in Micron's cash flow statements (2025 10-K Page 56).

Along with upfront costs, the rise of HBM (High Bandwidth Memory), often used in AI model training/datacenter applications, has made memory development ever more complex. No longer can you simply build more DRAM/NAND wafer capacity (basically expanding current manufacturing volume), as current supply now depends even more upon advanced and often non-standardizing packaging, with each AI system involving different parts of the supply chain (3D stacking, co-packaging with GPUs, etc.), making the entire system harder to scale.

Not to get too technical, but traditional DRAM often involves one chip (a flat piece of silicon) laid next to other chips on a 2D board. HBM, on the other hand, is comprised of multiple memory dies (chips) that are stacked vertically on top of each other, connected through something called TSVs (Through-Silicon Vias). This allows data to travel vertically rather than horizontally across a board, which means a shorter distance to travel, increasing bandwidth by large margins. However, these stacks also have no redundancy, meaning one bad stack will ruin the batch. This makes them exponentially harder to produce quickly and on a large scale, compared to traditional DRAM and earlier HBM models with lower dies, adding to the supply chain complexity.

Now, at first glance, this can seem like a revenue block, when, upon closer inspection, it is actually a boon to current manufacturers. Such blocks force the current oligopoly (Samsung, Micron, and SK Hynix) to put controls on supply, as they simply can't keep up with increasingly complex demand, and on tighter schedules. This, in turn, will stabilize prices, being one of the direct causes of the incoming AI earnings floor. A sustained shortage of products introduces a level of pricing power that will allow the durable margins showcased in my later valuation. Micron, and the others that dominate the market, now and increasingly so, have the power they need to elevate and sustain pricing, especially within non-discretionary and thus inelastic segments, giving them the prolonged revenue they need to justify current expectations.

Higher Margins Justify The Valuation And Known Re-Rating

The real question is, what do these more complex products truly mean for Micron and the semiconductor industry? It is known that AI will continue to grow, both externally in consumption and internally in intricacy, and that can be used to justify an even larger period of growth for Micron into the future. As the company continues to develop products with a higher level of value addition, we will continue to see margin expansion and stabilization outside of through-cycle ranges.

We no longer find ourselves in a semiconductor market dominated by consumer spending, and as I've described earlier, the mix shift that we've seen with AI is astronomical in its size, which is why I believe a stabilization in margins is completely warranted and deserved for Micron. Sure, sales growth will likely taper off beyond the triple-digit YoY growth we're likely to see in 2026, but the high-margin products (HBM) will likely stay steady as a relative part of revenue, providing Micron with the same insulation that other semiconductor companies have been receiving.

Pricing power is solidified and regained beyond that of original cycles, showing a shift upward beyond the original revenue booms/busts that we've seen beforehand. With AI-driven spending leading the way, I expect margins to stabilize and generate sustainable free cash flow that will allow Micron to reinvest and further saturate/control its market, satisfying investor expectations and justifying its valuation far into the future.

Financials

The financials of Micron have been generally volatile owing to the cyclical nature of its primary market, but most metrics have seen general improvements in recent years. In terms of profitability, margin expansion has been large and immediate, with EBITDA margins growing from 35.9% in 2024 to 49.1% in 2025, with the company on track to expand margins by another ~10% in 2026, owing to extraordinary revenue growth. FCF has also grown from just $182 million in 2024 to $1.6 billion in 2025, with a projected $12.88 billion in 2026. This goes to show the rapid, exponential growth seen in the semiconductor industry as a whole in recent years, as AI spending reaches new highs. While this growth will likely not last, durable margins and strong free cash flow conversion in later years (due to shrinking CapEx) will ensure sustained revenue even after the growth story is complete.

Going further into revenue, growth was strong throughout all segments in 2024 and 2025, with total YoY growth being 61.6% and 48.9% for both years, respectively. Of those numbers in 2025, the DRAM segment grew 62% YoY, and the NAND segment grew 18% YoY in 2025 (2025 10-K Page 52), representing strong AI/HBM demand across both segments as Micron sold out its inventory for 2025. Growth will be sustained by heavy CapEx as new fabs are built, as CapEx doubled in 2025 to $15.86 billion and is expected to again double or triple to ~$30-45 billion in 2026, marking the largest amount spent on CapEx in company history as Micron pushes to keep up with surging demand.

Balance sheet health is strong, with a net debt in the negatives (around ~-$2-4 billion due to large cash reserves) as Micron balances the need for heavy CapEx with a strong balance sheet. The company is heavily equity-financed as well, with a debt/equity ratio of just 0.13, which is a very conservative capital structure and one that will ensure company stability (along with a high FCF margin of ~40-50% driven by intense demand) and the ability to invest heavily and meet demand, just as it is doing currently. A healthy balance sheet and strong growth story set up the perfect storm for a long-term valuation, one that will be explained further in the segment below.

Valuation

Given the stock price of $496.72 as of market close on the 24th of April. I opted to use the exit multiple method in my approach, due in part to the cyclical nature of the semi market and the more defensible idea of a mid-cycle exit multiple rather than a perpetuity method that would imply a steady operating state. Using an exit multiple (EV/EBITDA) of 6.5x, meant to represent a high-end mid-cycle valuation, but also not so aggressive as to assume current demand will last in perpetuity, along with a calculated WACC of 11.15% (see calculation graph below), I reached an implied share price of $786.51, representing an upside of 58.3% from the current share price. Commenting further on the exit multiple, you will see similar forward multiples among comps (2026, 2027) when I explain that segment, and this was partially my guide for the decided exit multiple. Along with that, I felt it was necessary to avoid overstating a multiple that could imply a growth rate into perpetuity that was relatively unfeasible (5%-10%). To balance and remedy these issues, I arrived at 6.5x, a median between 5x (too low for current growth) and 8x-10x (hard to justify for long-term assumptions).

Revenue growth is guided towards current forward estimates for 2026 and 2027, with growth rates being 186.5% in 2026 and ~60% in 2027, tapering off heavily to mid-single digits by the end of the 10-year projection period. Margins remain largely stabilized with little compression/expansion, which is relatively bullish considering the cyclical nature of the market but justified if the theses play out as expected. CapEx remains elevated for the strongest 5 years of growth, tapering off heavily along with growth towards the end as we reach fab/market saturation and the need for heavy PP&E settles. Other than these notable changes, other margins/taxes remain largely in line with those of the historical period. Take revenue/line item numbers with a grain of salt as the terminal year is approached, as current growth implies an extremely high late-term revenue value that will possibly be outside of current/future possibilities.

Peers & Competition

The market in which Micron operates (memory/semiconductor space) is comprised largely of an oligopoly between the top 3 competitors, those being Samsung, Micron, and SK hynix, thus also being the largest in market cap and dwarfing the competition. With that being said, other competitors, though smaller, can be found, with the criterion for developing such a set being as follows:

  • Business Model/Primary Revenue Drivers
  • Market Capitalization
  • Forward Multiples (EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales)
  • Capital Structure
  • Margins (Gross/EBITDA)

All companies within the set operate in largely the same segments. Keep in mind that the business model was primary among the criteria, hence lower averages for market capitalization and the following enterprise value, although other multiples remain largely the same.

Micron follows most competitors very closely in terms of its forward multiples, with EV/EBITDA and EV/Sales following averages of 6.9x and 3.7x, with its own slightly discounted multiples of 5.7x and 3.4x, respectively (2027). This is possibly due to investors still valuing the company as a traditionally cyclical business, as the market has likely not priced in the AI earnings floor I spoke of earlier, and still considers earnings for Micron to be more volatile than its competitors, hinting at mispricing. This is strengthened, given the outperformance of the company's expected sales CAGR from 2025-2027, being nearly ~30-40% higher than the averages of the set. This, along with a lower guided P/E ratio, shows that the market is pricing in lower quality earnings than what other metrics are displaying, leaving the opportunity for appreciation when presented with this current disconnect.

Risks

Cycle reversal is undoubtedly the chief risk facing Micron right now. With a history of volatility, the chance that my idea of a less volatile incoming cycle not playing out represents the risk of extreme margin/growth compression for Micron in the future. Demand for AI is at an all-time high and is at possibly unsustainable levels of growth. If the currently aggressive CapEx schedule and increases in supply are not matched with a similar uptick in demand (10-K Page 12), the company risks missing expectations, cutting margins, and an unhealthy balance sheet if memory prices continue prior volatility trends.

I spoke of pricing power earlier in the article, and I want to revisit it with a similar risk. Micron and its competitors currently benefit from a tighter supply in terms of memory that grants them pricing power and margin expansion beyond any prior cycles, due to the advent of AI and HBM. This power comes with a similarly sized increase in the risk of these margins being the peak of what's to come. The long-term valuation of the company depends on its ability to price effectively and predictably, and if such power can't be maintained, the company risks margin normalization that could see EPS, net income, and other metrics systematically falling below investor expectations as we see margins stabilize at levels lower than expected.

Geopolitical and competitive risks also play a large role in the risk of oversupply. Chinese competitors (CXMT, YMTC), along with current rivals (Samsung, SK Hynix), are scaling at similar speeds and investing just as aggressively as Micron, threatening to leave them behind and innovate/develop new HBM products faster than Micron, eventually gaining market share and thus dulling the prospect of the current growth narrative. The pricing pressure and supply chain disruption risk that come with increased competition are something Micron will have to address as the market becomes more popular on a global scale.

Final Thoughts

Micron has already had an incredible run in the past year, and investors continue to place lofty expectations on accelerating growth. Although it is unlikely that current growth trends will continue, I see Micron continuing to stabilize margins and churning out sustainable free cash flow as current semiconductor cycles continue to evolve into an inelastic, AI-driven market. Micron is poised to capitalize even further on current trends, setting it up for growth that will continue to impress investors and drive long-term value and appreciation.

data.title: Markets In Context: Noise Vs. Fundamentals
data.description:
By Samuel Rines

Markets are being pulled in multiple directions. Geopolitical tensions, questions around Federal Reserve policy, and the rapid rise of artificial intelligence are all competing for investor attention.

But while the headlines suggest instability, the underlying picture is more balanced. Looking past short-term noise reveals a market environment supported by reasonable valuations, resilient economic data, and emerging long-term opportunities.

Geopolitics: Watching the Direction, Not the Headlines

The most important shift in geopolitics today isn’t the latest headline, it’s the broader trajectory. While tensions in the Middle East have created volatility, the persistence of ceasefires and the continuation of negotiations point to something more constructive.

Markets tend to respond not just to conflict, but to whether that conflict is escalating or stabilizing. The current environment suggests a move toward structured negotiations, particularly around key issues like nuclear policy and global energy routes. That shift matters.

For investors, this means focusing less on day-to-day developments and more on what resolution could unlock. Regions and sectors that were pressured during periods of uncertainty, such as international equities and value-oriented segments, may be positioned to recover as conditions stabilize.

Valuations and Earnings: Still on Solid Ground

Despite concerns that markets may be stretched, valuations remain close to long-term averages. The S&P 500 is trading around levels that can reasonably be described as fair value, rather than excessive.

At the same time, earnings continue to deliver. Expectations for double-digit growth highlight a market supported by fundamentals, not just sentiment. Technology remains the primary driver, but even here, valuations have moderated. Some of the largest companies tied to artificial intelligence are now trading at levels that look far more balanced than in prior years.

This creates an unusual dynamic: high-growth companies that are beginning to look like value opportunities, supported by both earnings strength and long-term demand.

A Potential Shift Beneath the Surface

While large-cap growth has dominated for years, there are signs that leadership may be broadening. Historically, periods of extreme divergence between growth and value tend to reverse over time.

Recent performance suggests that smaller-cap and value-oriented stocks are starting to regain traction. If this trend continues, it could mark the early stages of a multi-year rotation, one that rewards diversification beyond the largest names in the market.

Rates, Credit and the “New Normal”

Interest rates have settled into a range that feels unfamiliar to some investors but is historically quite normal. The 10-year Treasury yield hovering around 4% to 4.5% reflects an economy that is growing steadily, with inflation still above target but not accelerating.

The Federal Reserve appears to be nearing the end of its current policy cycle, with markets expecting limited movement from here. In this environment, chasing long-duration bonds has proven less effective. A more balanced approach to fixed income, focused on core exposure rather than reaching for duration, may be more appropriate.

Importantly, credit markets are not showing signs of systemic stress. While there have been pockets of volatility, funding markets remain stable, and spreads have behaved in a way that suggests opportunity rather than disruption.

The Consumer Story: Stronger Than Expected

One of the biggest concerns has been whether higher energy prices could derail the U.S. consumer. So far, the evidence suggests otherwise.

Spending remains solid, and corporate results continue to reflect healthy demand. While gasoline prices attract attention, their impact on overall consumption appears more limited than many assume. Higher wages and improved efficiency have helped offset the pressure.

This resilience is a key reason why the broader economy continues to avoid the recession many had expected.

AI: Productivity Over Displacement

Artificial intelligence remains a defining theme, but the narrative is evolving. Rather than replacing workers outright, AI is increasingly being used to enhance productivity.

Companies are investing heavily, and early results show tangible benefits, more efficient operations, improved output and stronger revenue potential. In many cases, AI is enabling growth rather than constraining it.

While disruption is inevitable, the more immediate effect may be expansion: more tools, more innovation and, ultimately, more demand for skilled labor.

Conclusion

Markets today are navigating uncertainty, but they are not without direction. Beneath the surface, valuations are reasonable, earnings are strong, and the macro backdrop remains stable.

Geopolitics, AI and monetary policy will continue to shape the landscape, but they also create opportunities. For investors willing to look beyond the headlines, the environment remains constructive, especially over a longer-term horizon.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy ”.
